entries.symptom: Thermostat screen is completely blank — no display, no response to any button. HVAC system is fully dead (no heat, no cool, no fan). Breaker is on. Other circuits in the house work fine.
entries.diagnosis: Blown 3A or 5A fuse on the furnace/air-handler control board. The thermostat gets its 24V power from the R (red) wire — if the board's transformer fuse blows, the thermostat loses power. Common cause: a shorted thermostat wire (pinched during renovation, critter damage) or a miswired thermostat swap.
entries.part_number: 3A automotive blade fuse (ATC/ATO type) — keep spares on the truck. If fuse blows repeatedly, trace the short.
procedure: At the air handler / furnace, kill power at the switch.
Open the control board compartment. Locate the low-voltage fuse — typically a small automotive blade fuse (3A or 5A, purple or tan) near the transformer.
Pull the fuse and test with a multimeter for continuity. If open = blown fuse.

Before replacing: disconnect the R (red) wire from the board terminal strip. Measure resistance from R to C at the thermostat end — should be open (infinite). If it reads near 0 ohms, there's a short in the thermostat cable.
Inspect thermostat wiring at both ends for pinched, nicked, or touching conductors. Fix any short found.
If the wiring checks out, install a new 3A fuse (match the original rating — do NOT upsize).
Restore power. Verify 24–28V AC between R and C at the thermostat.
The thermostat should power on within 5 seconds. If it does, run a test cycle (heat or cool).
If the fuse blows again immediately, the short is still present — check the outdoor unit wiring, contactor coil, and zone damper motors.
safety_notes: Control boards can have 120V or 240V line voltage on the same board as the 24V section — power must be off before touching.
Never replace a 3A fuse with a higher-rated fuse — the fuse protects the transformer, which is expensive to replace.
If there's evidence of arcing or burn marks on the board, the board itself may need replacement.

entries.symptom: Outdoor unit is running but the condenser fan is not spinning. Compressor is hot. High-side pressure is climbing above 450 PSI. Unit may trip on high-pressure cutout within a few minutes of starting.
entries.diagnosis: Seized or burned-out condenser fan motor. Bearings failed — motor shaft won't turn by hand. Often caused by running the unit with debris restricting the fan blade or after a power surge.
entries.part_number: HC39GE237 (Carrier 1/4 HP condenser fan motor, 1075 RPM, 208-230V)
procedure: Kill power at the disconnect immediately — running the compressor without condenser airflow causes extreme high-side pressure and can damage the compressor.
Try spinning the fan blade by hand (through the fan guard, carefully). If it won't turn or feels gritty, motor bearings are gone.
Remove the fan guard (4 screws on Carrier 24ACC6). Lift it off with the blade still attached.
Disconnect the motor leads from the contactor — typically a 3-wire connection (brown = hot, purple = capacitor, ground).
Remove the motor mounting bolts (4 bolts through the motor bracket to the condenser top plate).
Transfer the fan blade to the new motor: loosen the Allen set screw on the hub. Note the blade height — typically 1" below the fan guard plane.
Mount the new motor, reconnect leads. Verify the capacitor rating matches (typically 5 µF for 1/4 HP motor).
Reinstall the fan guard. Hand-spin to check for clearance.
Restore power. Verify fan starts when thermostat calls. Check motor amp draw — should be below 1.8A.
Monitor high-side pressure — should drop to 300–350 PSI range within 5 minutes.
safety_notes: High-side pressure above 450 PSI is dangerous — do not run the unit. Kill power immediately if you see this.
The compressor may be extremely hot after running without fan — don't touch the discharge line.
Fan blades can start unexpectedly if power is restored — always disconnect before working near the blade.

entries.symptom: Intermittent cooling with audible clicking from the outdoor unit. Compressor attempts to start then trips out after a few seconds. Short cycling pattern.
entries.diagnosis: Failed or weak dual-run capacitor. Compressor cannot achieve starting torque, trips internal overload.
procedure: Kill power at the disconnect and the breaker. Verify with non-contact tester.
Remove the outdoor unit access panel (4 sheet-metal screws).
Discharge the existing capacitor across HERM and C terminals with an insulated 20k ohm resistor.
Note wire colors: Yellow to HERM (compressor), Brown to FAN, Black to C (common).
Measure capacitance on both sides with a multimeter in capacitance mode. Spec: 45 uF (HERM) / 5 uF (FAN), ±6%.
If either side reads >6% off spec, replace with P291-4554RS (45/5 uF 440V).
Reconnect wires to matching terminals. Secure with the original retaining bracket.
Restore power. Verify compressor starts without clicking; listen for 3 full cooling cycles.
Confirm supply air temp drop of 15-20°F across the air handler.
safety_notes: Capacitors store lethal voltage even when power is off — always discharge before touching terminals.
Verify disconnect is pulled AND breaker is off. Tag if needed.
Bulging or leaking capacitors indicate failure; handle with gloves.

entries.symptom: Unit runs constantly but house won't cool below 80°F. Suction line is warm, not sweating. Liquid line feels hot. Possible hissing near the service valve or coil.
entries.diagnosis: Low R-410A charge due to slow leak — likely at the service valve Schrader core or a brazed joint on the evaporator coil. Subcooling will read low (<5°F vs. 10–12°F spec), superheat high (>20°F).
entries.part_number: R-410A refrigerant (sold by weight) + leak sealant if pinhole, or coil replacement if severe
procedure: Connect gauges to the high and low service ports with a manifold set rated for R-410A (660 PSI burst).
Record suction pressure and liquid pressure. Expected: ~118 PSI suction / ~350 PSI liquid at 95°F ambient.
Calculate subcooling (condenser sat temp minus liquid line temp) and superheat (suction line temp minus evaporator sat temp).
If subcooling < 5°F and superheat > 15°F, unit is undercharged.
Perform a nitrogen leak test: isolate the system, pressurize to 150 PSI with dry nitrogen, soap-bubble all joints and Schrader cores.
If leak found at Schrader core, replace the core with a 1/4" R-410A rated core (under pressure if using a core removal tool).
If leak is at a brazed joint, recover refrigerant, braze repair, pressure test, evacuate to 500 microns, and recharge by weight per the data plate.
Weigh in refrigerant per nameplate charge (typically 5 lbs 12 oz for GSX130361). Verify subcooling hits 10–12°F.
Run unit for 15 minutes, re-check pressures and subcooling. Confirm supply air is 55–60°F.
safety_notes: R-410A operates at significantly higher pressures than R-22 — always use 410A-rated hoses, gauges, and recovery equipment.
Never vent refrigerant to atmosphere — EPA Section 608 violation.
Wear safety glasses when connecting gauges — liquid slugging can cause frostbite.
If you suspect a large leak (>30% charge lost), check compressor oil level before recharging.

entries.symptom: Furnace ignites briefly (4-7 seconds) then shuts down. Blower continues. No heat. Often presents as intermittent no-heat, worse after a humid day.
entries.diagnosis: Dirty or failing flame sensor. Oxide buildup prevents the board from detecting flame, triggering flame-loss shutdown.
procedure: Kill power at the furnace switch.
Remove the burner compartment cover.
Locate the flame sensor — single metal rod with a ceramic insulator, mounted at the burner tray, opposite the ignitor.
Remove the 1/4" hex-head screw holding the sensor bracket. Pull the sensor out carefully.
Disconnect the single sensor wire from the spade terminal.
Clean the sensor rod with fine steel wool or 400-grit emery cloth — light pressure, clean until dull metal is uniform. Do NOT sand the ceramic.
Wipe with a dry cloth. Reinstall in the same position.
Restore power and run a heat cycle. Flame should establish and stay lit. Blower should run after the fan-on delay.
If cleaning doesn't resolve the issue in the next cycle, replace with Lennox 10K5601.
safety_notes: Power must be OFF — control board can energize the ignitor unexpectedly during diagnostics.
Gas supply should be off at the appliance shutoff before removing the burner cover.
If you smell gas at any point, stop, ventilate, and flag_safety.

entries.symptom: Unit runs but cooling is poor. Suction line is frosted back to the compressor. Evaporator coil is partially iced. Superheat reads 2–4°F (far too low — spec is 8–12°F). Subcooling is normal or high.
entries.diagnosis: TXV (thermostatic expansion valve) stuck open — overfeeding refrigerant to the evaporator. The sensing bulb may have lost its charge, or the valve itself is mechanically stuck. Flooding the compressor with liquid refrigerant — can cause slugging damage if not addressed.
entries.part_number: R-410A TXV (aftermarket: Sporlan EBSE-5-GA or OEM Lennox 80W47) — must match tonnage and refrigerant
procedure: Let the unit thaw completely before diagnostics — run the fan only for 20–30 minutes if iced.
Connect gauges. Run the system and measure superheat at the suction line near the compressor.
Normal superheat for this unit: 8–12°F. If reading <5°F, the TXV is overfeeding.
Check the TXV sensing bulb — it should be securely strapped to the suction line at the 10 o'clock or 2 o'clock position, insulated from ambient air. A loose bulb can cause erratic behavior.
If the bulb is properly mounted and superheat is still too low, the TXV is stuck open internally.
Tap the TXV body with a wrench handle — occasionally this frees a stuck valve (temporary fix only).
For permanent repair: recover refrigerant, braze out the old TXV, install the replacement (Sporlan EBSE-5-GA or Lennox 80W47).
Mount the new sensing bulb at the correct position on the suction line. Insulate it.
Pressure test, evacuate to 500 microns, recharge by weight.
Run the system and verify superheat is in the 8–12°F range. Monitor for 15 minutes for stability.
safety_notes: Liquid refrigerant flooding back to the compressor can cause mechanical failure (slugging) — if superheat is near 0°F, shut the unit down immediately.
Brazing near the evaporator coil requires nitrogen purge to prevent oxide formation inside the lines.
If the coil is severely iced, water will drip during thaw — protect electrical components and the blower motor below.

entries.symptom: Furnace fires and heats the heat exchanger, but no air comes through the vents. Blower motor hums but doesn't spin. Limit switch trips after 2–3 minutes, shutting down the burners. High-temp smell from the plenum.
entries.diagnosis: Failed blower motor (PSC type). Motor winding is partially shorted — draws locked rotor amps but can't turn. Run capacitor may test good. Common after 10–15 years, especially in dusty environments.
entries.part_number: 51-23012-41 (Rheem 1/2 HP PSC blower motor) — universal 5.6" diameter PSC motor also fits
procedure: Kill power at the furnace disconnect switch and breaker.
Remove the blower compartment door (bottom panel on RGPH).
Disconnect the motor wiring harness — note the wire colors and speed tap used (typically black = high, blue = medium-high).
Remove the blower assembly: two 5/16" bolts on the blower rail. Slide the whole assembly out.
Remove the motor from the blower wheel: loosen the 5/16" set screw on the wheel hub. May need penetrating oil if corroded.
Check the run capacitor with a multimeter — if it reads within ±6% of rated µF, motor is the failure. If cap is bad, replace both.
Install the new motor onto the blower wheel. Tighten set screw. Align wheel so it doesn't rub the housing.
Reconnect wiring to the same speed tap. Reinstall the blower assembly.
Restore power and run a heat cycle. Verify airflow at the registers and confirm the limit switch doesn't trip.
Measure amp draw on the motor — should be at or below nameplate FLA.
safety_notes: The heat exchanger will be extremely hot if the blower was off during a firing cycle — wait 10 minutes before reaching into the blower compartment.
Blower wheels can have sharp edges — wear gloves.
If the limit switch tripped multiple times, inspect the heat exchanger for cracks (soot marks, flame rollout signs) before reassembling.

entries.symptom: Outdoor unit does not energize when thermostat calls for cooling. Indoor blower runs. 24V control voltage present at Y terminal but compressor and fan stay off.
entries.diagnosis: Pitted, stuck, or failed 24V contactor. Common on Trane XR14 after 5-8 years in humid climates.
procedure: Kill power at the disconnect and verify with a tester.
Open the control compartment on the condenser.
Visually inspect the contactor — pitting, burn marks, or welded contacts are failure signs.
Check the 24V coil for continuity (should read ~10-20 ohms). Open coil = replace.
With power restored, energize the thermostat call for cool and listen for a definitive 'click' at the contactor. No click with 24V present at the coil = failed contactor.
Kill power again. Label and remove line-voltage leads (L1, L2) and low-voltage leads.
Swap in CTR02266 (2-pole, 30A, 24V coil). Reconnect leads in the same positions.
Restore power, test cooling cycle. Confirm contactor pulls in cleanly and compressor starts.
safety_notes: Line voltage is present on the contactor's top lugs even with the disconnect pulled if the unit is still wired to a live breaker — check BOTH.
Never bypass a contactor by jumpering L1 to T1 as a diagnostic — hazardous and can damage the compressor.

entries.symptom: Furnace inducer motor runs but burners never ignite. LED error code 33 (pressure switch stuck open). Inducer sounds normal. Flue and intake pipes appear clear from outside.
entries.diagnosis: Pressure switch cannot close because of a blocked condensate drain trap. The XV80 is a 80% AFUE furnace but uses a condensate trap in the collector box — when clogged, negative pressure can't build in the vestibule, and the switch stays open. Less commonly: cracked pressure switch hose, or failed switch.
entries.part_number: SWT02523 (Trane pressure switch) — but usually fixing the drain resolves it
procedure: Kill power at the furnace switch.
Locate the pressure switch — small round disc with a diaphragm, mounted near the inducer. Has a rubber hose running to the collector box.
Inspect the hose for cracks, kinks, or moisture inside. Replace if damaged (1/4" ID silicone hose).
Remove the condensate drain hose from the collector box. Blow through it — if blocked, clear with compressed air or a pipe cleaner.
Remove the collector box cover (if accessible) and clear any sludge or debris from the trap.
Pour a small amount of water into the trap to re-prime it (traps must hold water to seal).
With the hose reconnected, restore power. Let the inducer run and listen for the pressure switch to click closed (audible click within 15–30 seconds of inducer start).
If the switch still won't close: jumper it briefly (for diagnosis ONLY, never leave jumped) to verify the rest of the ignition sequence works. If it fires, the switch or its port is the issue.
Replace the pressure switch (SWT02523) if cleaning doesn't resolve. Verify the new switch closes within the inducer pre-purge period.
safety_notes: Never leave a pressure switch jumped — it exists to prevent combustion gas spillage into the living space.
If the inducer motor sounds labored or different than normal, check for a cracked heat exchanger before proceeding.
Condensate from a furnace can be mildly acidic — avoid skin contact and dispose properly.

entries.symptom: Outdoor unit contactor pulls in but compressor does not start. Condenser fan runs. Loud humming or buzzing from the compressor for 5–10 seconds, then the circuit breaker trips or the overload clicks off. Unit is hot to the touch near the compressor.
entries.diagnosis: Compressor with open or shorted winding, or mechanically locked rotor. Check winding resistance: C-R + C-S should equal R-S (within 2 ohms). Mega-ohm test to ground — <1 MΩ = grounded winding. If windings test OK, compressor is mechanically seized (internal bearing failure).
entries.part_number: S1-01504270000 (York/JCI scroll compressor, 3-ton) — requires recovery, brazing, vacuum, and recharge
procedure: Kill power at the disconnect. Wait 5 minutes for the internal overload to reset.
Remove the compressor terminal cover. Inspect for burn marks or melted plastic — indicates electrical failure.
Using a multimeter on R×1 scale, measure winding resistance between all three terminals: C-R, C-S, R-S.
C-R + C-S should approximately equal R-S. If any winding reads open (OL) or very low (<0.5 ohm), the compressor is electrically failed.
Mega-ohm test: measure from each terminal to the compressor shell/ground. Reading <1 MΩ = grounded winding — compressor must be replaced.
If windings test OK: try a hard-start kit (potential relay + start capacitor). If it starts, the motor is weak but functional — recommend replacement anyway.
If compressor is confirmed failed: recover all refrigerant, cut and braze the suction and discharge lines, remove the old compressor (heavy — use a dolly).
Install replacement compressor, braze connections, pressure test with nitrogen, evacuate to 500 microns, recharge by weight.
Add correct oil charge (POE oil, check spec — typically 32 oz for this unit).
Start the unit, check amp draw against nameplate RLA/LRA. Monitor for 30 minutes.
safety_notes: Compressor terminals carry line voltage — verify power is dead before removing the terminal cover.
A grounded compressor can energize the chassis — always test before touching.
Compressor replacement involves brazing under nitrogen — fire extinguisher must be on site.
This is a major repair — recommend quoting the customer before proceeding. Flag scope change if original work order was for a different issue.
